decks.name: Consonant Rules
decks.description: Rules 1-8: How consonants behave in English spelling

cards.front: Rule 1: C before E, I, Y
cards.back:
ZERO exceptions! C before E, I, Y = always /s/. Otherwise, C says /k/.

Examples: CITY, CEILING, CYCLE, CANCEL
cards.hint: Think: ICE, CELL, CYAN — all soft C

cards.front: Rule 2: G before E, I, Y
cards.back:
G TENDS to soften before E, I, Y — but has exceptions unlike C. G may say /j/ before E, I, Y. Otherwise, G always says /g/.

Examples: GIRAFFE, GENERATE, GYMNASIUM
cards.hint: Unlike C, G has exceptions: GET, GIFT, GIRL stay hard /g/

cards.front: Rule 3: I, U, V, J never end alone
cards.back:
English words do not end in I, U, V, or J. A companion letter is added.

Examples: BLUE (not BLU), GIVE (not GIV), FLY (I→Y), BRIDGE (J→DGE)
cards.hint: These four shy letters always need a friend at the end

cards.front: Rule 4: Double S after single vowel
cards.back:
After a single vowel, a base word does not end in S alone. It doubles to SS.

Examples: MISS, BOSS, FUSS, DRESS
cards.hint: Exceptions: BUS, GAS, YES, THIS

cards.front: Rule 5: Double F, L, S, Z
cards.back:
In a one-syllable word, after a single short vowel, final /f/, /l/, /s/, or /z/ is doubled.

Examples: STAFF, BELL, BUZZ, SKILL
cards.hint: Short vowel + F, L, S, or Z at the end? Double it up!

cards.back:
CK is used right after a single short vowel at the end of a one-syllable word.

Examples: BACK, NECK, TRICK, CLOCK
cards.hint: Long vowel or consonant before? Just use K: BAKE, MILK

cards.back:
TCH is used right after a single short vowel.

Examples: MATCH, FETCH, STITCH, CLUTCH
cards.hint: Exceptions: SUCH, MUCH, RICH, WHICH

cards.back:
DGE is used right after a single short vowel.

Examples: BADGE, LEDGE, BRIDGE, FUDGE
cards.hint: Long vowel? Just use GE: CAGE, HUGE, STAGE

decks.name: Vowel Rules
decks.description: Rules 9-11: How vowels pair up and Q's partnership

cards.front: Rule 9: AY at end, AI in middle
cards.back:
AY is used at the end of a base word for /ay/. AI is used everywhere else.

Examples: PLAY (end), RAIN (middle), STAY (end), WAIT (middle)
cards.hint: End of the road? AY. Middle of the word? AI.

cards.front: Rule 10: OY at end, OI in middle
cards.back:
OY is used at the end of a base word for /oi/. OI is used everywhere else.

Examples: BOY (end), JOIN (middle), ENJOY (end), COIN (middle)
cards.hint: Same pattern as AY/AI — position matters

cards.back:
Q always appears with U. QU says /kw/. No English word has Q without U.

Examples: QUEEN, QUARTER, BANQUET, TRANQUIL
cards.hint: Q and U are inseparable best friends

decks.name: Suffix Rules
decks.description: Rules 12-16, 22-28: How words change when adding suffixes

cards.back:
Drop the silent E when adding a suffix that starts with a vowel.

Examples: BAKING, HOPING, WRITING, DANCING
cards.hint: Vowel suffix coming? Silent E steps aside

cards.back:
Keep the silent E when adding a suffix that starts with a consonant.

Examples: HOPEFUL, CARELESS, MOVEMENT, SAFETY
cards.hint: Consonant suffix coming? Silent E stays put

cards.back:
Double the final consonant before a vowel suffix when the word ends in 1 vowel + 1 consonant (stressed).

Examples: RUNNING, STOPPING, BEGINNING, ADMITTED
cards.hint: Stressed ending + 1 vowel + 1 consonant? Double up!

cards.back:
Consonant + Y at the end? Change Y to I before any suffix (except suffixes starting with I).

Examples: HAPPINESS, CARRIED, BEAUTIFUL, STUDIED
cards.hint: But keep Y before -ING: carrying, studying

cards.back:
Vowel + Y at the end? Keep the Y when adding any suffix.

Examples: PLAYING, ENJOYMENT, STAYING, DELAYED
cards.hint: Vowel + Y = best friends. Never separate them.

cards.back:
The suffix -ED has 3 sounds: /ed/ after T or D, /d/ after voiced, /t/ after unvoiced.

Examples: PLANTED (/ed/), CALLED (/d/), JUMPED (/t/)
cards.hint: Listen to the last sound of the base word

cards.back:
Add -ES (not just -S) when a word ends in S, X, Z, CH, or SH.

Examples: BOXES, BRUSHES, CHURCHES, GLASSES
cards.hint: If the ending already hisses or buzzes, -ES adds a syllable

cards.back:
Consonant + Y at the end? Change Y to I and add -ES to make it plural.

Examples: BABIES, CITIES, FAMILIES, STORIES
cards.hint: But vowel + Y? Just add S: MONKEYS, KEYS, DAYS

cards.back:
Both say /shun/. -TION follows consonants/long vowels. -SION follows other patterns.

Examples: NATION, MOTION, TENSION, DECISION
cards.hint: -TION is far more common; -SION is the specialist

cards.back:
-ABLE joins complete words. -IBLE joins roots that can't stand alone.

Examples: COMFORTABLE (comfort+able), VISIBLE (vis+ible)
cards.hint: Can the base stand alone? ABLE. Can't? IBLE.

cards.back:
The suffix -AL turns a noun or verb into an adjective.

Examples: ACCIDENTAL, EMOTIONAL, TRADITIONAL, MUSICAL
cards.hint: Need an adjective? Slap on -AL

cards.back:
Add -LY to adjectives to make adverbs. If the adjective ends in L, the result is -LLY.

Examples: QUICKLY, CAREFULLY, USUALLY, FINALLY
cards.hint: L + LY = LLY — that double L is correct!

decks.name: Silent E Rules
decks.description: Rules 17-21: The 5 jobs of silent E

cards.front: Rule 17: Silent E Job 1 — Long Vowel
cards.back:
Silent E makes the preceding vowel say its long sound (its name).

Examples: BAKE (A says /ay/), THEME (E says /ee/), DRIVE (I says /eye/), FLUTE (U says /oo/)
cards.hint: The most famous E job — vowel says its own name

cards.front: Rule 18: Silent E Job 2 — V/U Guard
cards.back:
English words do not end in V or U, so E follows them.

Examples: GIVE, HAVE, BLUE, TRUE
cards.hint: V and U are shy — they need E to stand at the end

cards.front: Rule 19: Silent E Job 3 — Soft C/G
cards.back:
Silent E keeps C and G soft (/s/ and /j/) at the end of a word.

Examples: DANCE, PRINCE, LARGE, CHARGE
cards.hint: Without the E, C would snap to /k/ and G would snap to /g/

cards.front: Rule 20: Silent E Job 4 — Syllable Vowel
cards.back:
Every syllable needs a vowel. E provides one where needed (like the -LE ending).

Examples: CASTLE, BOTTLE, TABLE, PEOPLE
cards.hint: The -LE ending would be vowel-less without that E

cards.front: Rule 21: Silent E Job 5 — Not Plural
cards.back:
Silent E prevents a word from looking like a plural ending in S.

Examples: HOUSE, GOOSE, MOOSE, PROMISE
cards.hint: Without E, HOUSE would be HOUS — looks like a weird plural!

decks.name: Shield Rules
decks.description: Rules 29-30: How letters protect short vowels

cards.front: Rule 29: I shields C and G
cards.back:
The letter I in a suffix keeps C soft (/s/) and G soft (/j/) in the base word.

Examples: DANCING, CHANGING, NOTICING, MANAGING
cards.hint: The I in -ING acts like a shield for soft C and G

cards.back:
After a short vowel: use CK, DGE, TCH. After anything else (long vowel, consonant, two vowels): use K, GE, CH.

Examples: BACK vs BAKE, BADGE vs CAGE, MATCH vs MARCH
cards.hint: The shield trio protects short vowels

decks.name: Advanced Rules
decks.description: Rules 31-38: Advanced patterns, silent letters, and borrowed words

cards.front: Rule 31: O before M, N, V, TH
cards.back:
O says /u/ when followed by M, N, V, or TH.

Examples: MOTHER, MONEY, LOVE, MONTH
cards.hint: LOVE rhymes with DOVE, not STOVE

cards.back:
When vowel-consonant-E doesn't make a long vowel, the E has a different silent E job (2-5).

Examples: HAVE (Job 2), DANCE (Job 3), GIVE (Job 2), LOVE (Job 2)
cards.hint: Not every final E makes a long vowel — check other jobs

cards.back:
Five silent pairs: KN (K silent), WR (W silent), GN (G silent), MB (B silent), MN (N silent).

Examples: KNEE, WRITE, GNOME, THUMB
cards.hint: The first letter hides but stays in spelling

cards.back:
More silent L patterns: LK — L is silent. LM — L is silent.

Examples: WALK, TALK, CALM, PALM
cards.hint: But L IS pronounced in MILK, SILK, FILM

cards.back:
After W, the letter A often says /o/ instead of /a/.

Examples: WATCH, WANT, WATER, WASP
cards.hint: W is a vowel-warper — bends A toward /o/

cards.back:
L modifies certain vowels: ALL = /awl/, OLL = /ohl/, ULL = /ool/.

Examples: BALL, ROLL, FULL, INSTALL
cards.hint: L shifts ALL, OLL, and ULL into unique sounds

cards.back:
The pattern -IGHT always says /eye-t/. GH is silent in this pattern.

Examples: LIGHT, NIGHT, BRIGHT, FLIGHT
cards.hint: One of the most reliable patterns in English

cards.back:
Borrowed words keep their original spelling patterns: Greek PH, CH; French CH; Latin TI.

Examples: PHILOSOPHY, CHARACTER, CHAMPAGNE, PARTITION
cards.hint: Weird spelling? Check the passport — Greek, French, or Latin
